coredata.dc:title: Aggregation-induced color fine-tunable carbon dot phosphorescence covering from green to near-infrared for advanced information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qijun
Cheng, Dengke
Gu, Hailing
Yang, Daiqi
Li, Yuchen
Meng, Shuai
Zhao, Yunyang
Tang, Zikang
Zhang, YiBo
Tan, Jing
Qu, Songnan
coredata.dc:description: Multi-color phosphorescence carbon dots (CDs) show enormous potential in advanced information encryption, yet the achievement of wide-range, fine-tunable phosphorescence CDs, especially with near-infrared (NIR) phosphorescence emission, confront numerous challenges. Herein, for the first time, the phosphorescence CD-based composites with fine-color tunable property ranging from green to NIR are obtained via regulating CDs contents in annealed boric acid (BA). The synthesized CDs are fully passivated by BA molecular and form uniform surface state, which avoids aggregation-induced quenching to some extent. By increasing the CDs contents in BA during thermal treatment, the aggregation degree of CDs is gradually increased, causing substantial electronic interactions. This leads to energy splitting and form low-energy aggregation states, resulting in phosphorescence continuously shifts from 530 to 555, 585, 625, 645 and 750 nm. Based on aggregation-induced discoloration, we further explore solvent-triggered evolutionary discoloration property of CDs, that is, trace methanol could induce phosphorescence color change from green to red, and illustrate their potential applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Near-infrared phosphorescence
Color-tunable phosphorescence
Dynamic multimode encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002152, 62005106 and 61922091), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Zhenjiang-Modern Agriculture (NY2021007), the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0040/2019/A1, 0128/2020/A3, 0131.2020.A3 and 0073/2019/AMJ), Fund from the University of Macau (SRG2019-00163-IAPME), Special fund for Science and technology innovation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022610) and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (Grant No.KYCX22_3629). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Weiwei from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002152, 62005106 and 61922091), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Zhenjiang-Modern Agriculture (NY2021007), the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0040/2019/A1, 0128/2020/A3, 0131.2020.A3 and 0073/2019/AMJ), Fund from the University of Macau (SRG2019-00163-IAPME), Special fund for Science and technology innovation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022610) and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (Grant No.KYCX22_3629). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Weiwei from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the preparation of the CDs@BA composites. (b and c) Phosphorescence spectra and CIE 1931 coordinates of CDs@BA composites.

(a-d) TEM images of the G-CDs, Y-CDs, R-CDs and NIR-CDs, and the inset is the corresponding size distribution. (e) FTIR spectra of the G-CDs, Y-CDs, R-CDs and NIR-CDs. (f) XPS full-survey spectra of the G-CDs, Y-CDs, R-CDs and NIR-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@BA composites. (b-e) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs@BA composites. (f) Phosphorescence decay spectra of the G-CDs@BA (λex = 365 nm and λem = 530 nm), Y-CDs@BA (λex = 365 nm and λem = 560 nm), R-CDs@BA (λex = 400 nm and λem = 650 nm), and NIR-CDs@BA composites (λex = 550 nm and λem = 750 nm).
Possible phosphorescence emission processes and energy level diagram of multi-color phosphorescence CDs@BA composites.

(a) Phosphorescent and (c) fluorescent spectra (under 425 nm excitation) of G-CDs@BA powders before and after spraying methanol. (b) phosphorescent and (d) fluorescent photographs of G-CDs@BA powders before (top) and after (bottom) spraying methanol. (e) Multicolor phosphorescent photographs for advanced dynamic information encryption.

head.ce:title: Aggregation-induced color fine-tunable carbon dot phosphorescence covering from green to near-infrared for advanced information encryption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-color phosphorescence carbon dots (CDs) show enormous potential in advanced information encryption, yet the achievement of wide-range, fine-tunable phosphorescence CDs, especially with near-infrared (NIR) phosphorescence emission, confront numerous challenges. Herein, for the first time, the phosphorescence CD-based composites with fine-color tunable property ranging from green to NIR are obtained via regulating CDs contents in annealed boric acid (BA). The synthesized CDs are fully passivated by BA molecular and form uniform surface state, which avoids aggregation-induced quenching to some extent. By increasing the CDs contents in BA during thermal treatment, the aggregation degree of CDs is gradually increased, causing substantial electronic interactions. This leads to energy splitting and form low-energy aggregation states, resulting in phosphorescence continuously shifts from 530 to 555, 585, 625, 645 and 750 nm. Based on aggregation-induced discoloration, we further explore solvent-triggered evolutionary discoloration property of CDs, that is, trace methanol could induce phosphorescence color change from green to red, and illustrate their potential applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Multi-color afterglow materials have received considerable attention for their potential applications in data security, optoelectronics and biological imaging [1–3]. Carbon dots (CDs) as an outstanding nano-luminescent material have aroused tremendous interest because of their low cost, easy preparation process, and good photostability [4–16]. To date, afterglow emission of CDs including phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence have been explored [17,18] and efficient blue, green, and red emissions have been achieved in several CD systems [19–25]. However, most multicolor afterglow of CDs is still centered in the short wavelength (<600 nm) visible region, and there remains a quite challenging to realize color wide-range and fine-tuning afterglow of CD-based materials. Especially, near-infrared (NIR) afterglow emission (>700 nm) with invisibility/large penetration depths shows a significant scientific value in information security and bio-imaging [26–32], however, to our knowledge, CDs with such property has rarely reported yet.

To achieve multi-color afterglow, several rational strategies have been proposed to regulate the bandgap of CDs based on the radiative energy transfer, carbonization degree modulation, and doped graphitic nitrogen [33–35]. For example, Zhuang and co-workers [33] prepared rare earth/CDs composites with cyan, green, or orange emission based on radiative energy transfer method. By rationally selecting different types of emitters to match CDs, energy resonance transfer can occur between them, resulting in multi-color afterglow. However, it is hard to realize fine-tuning afterglow due to the stringent requirement of spectral overlap and the interactions between the energy donors and acceptors. Recently, Liu et al.[34] developed a kind of multi-color CDs@SiO2 composites with blue to red afterglow emission by controlling the carbonization degree of CDs. Enlarging the carbonization degree of CDs can lead to longer wavelength afterglow emissions. However, the high carbonization degree of CDs usually leads to strong non-radiative transitions, making NIR emissions difficult to obtain.

Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) proposed by Tang’s group has attracted great research interest and an effective strategy to modulate luminescence by utilizing aggregation [36,37]. Unlike the aggregation-caused-quenching, AIE are non-emissive or weakly emissive as isolated species but appear brightly luminescent upon aggregation. Recently, aggregation-induced phosphorescence has been achieved based on various material systems [38–42]. As the degree of aggregation increases, energy levels can broaden and split into more channels, which is helpful to obtain wide-range and fine-tuning afterglow. However, the CDs usually contain a large number of surface defects, nonradiative losses usually occur during their aggregation, resulting in that aggregation-induced wide-range tunable CD phosphorescence emission is rarely reported.

In this work, we reported a kind of phosphorescence color fine-tunable CD material based on aggregation-induced approach. The CDs were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal treatment of lycorine, and then composited with BA at different content through thermal melting. During thermal treatment progress, CDs are fully passivated with annealed BA, resulting in a uniform surface state that avoids aggregation-induced quenching to some content. With the increasing of CD contents in annealed BA, the distance between CDs is decreased, which causes substantial electronic interactions. As a result, energy levels can broaden and split into more channels, resulting in phosphorescence continuously shifts from green to NIR color. To the best of our knowledge, this is very rare and unusual phenomenon in the CD phosphorescence materials. Furthermore, we design a novel phosphorescence color-tunable CD system with dynamic evolution under methanol solvent triggering. Trace solvent can effectively induce phosphorescent color change from green to red due to solvent-induced CDs aggregation. Based on these unique properties, advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption methodologies are demonstrated.

A fabrication method of wide color-tunable phosphorescence CD materials is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Frist, the CDs were synthesized by one‐pot hydrothermal treatment of lycorine at 200 °C for 2 h, and then composited with BA substances at different concentrations under heat-melt treatment (Fig. 1a). It is interesting that as the mass ratio between CDs and BA increased, the phosphorescence wavelength can be continuously customized from 530, 555, 585, 625, 645 to 750 nm (Fig. 1b), achieving ultra-wide emission coverage from green, to yellow, to orange, to red, and to NIR. Correspondingly, the phosphorescence commission internationale eclairage (CIE) coordinates are plotted in Fig. 1c. The color coordinates of the CDs@BA composites are (0.38,0.57), (0.43,0.54), (0.49,0.48), (0.54,0.44), (0.55,0.44) and (0.64,0.36), respectively, indicating color wide-range and fine-tuning properties. To investigate the multicolor emission, four typical CDs@BA composites exhibiting green, yellow, red and NIR afterglow are selected for further characterizations, which are labeled as G-CDs@BA, Y-CDs@BA, R-CDs@BA and NIR-CDs@BA.

The four CDs were extracted from four typical CDs@BA composites, similar to previous report [43] and used further characterizations. TEM images (Fig. 2 a-d) exhibit that four typical CDs are quasi-spherical and homogeneous nanodots with average mean sizes of ∼ 8.5 nm. There is little significant change in the size of the CDs for the selected four samples. The chemical structure and composition of the four CDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2e), the absorption bands centered at 3400, 1631 and 1617 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of O − H, C = O and C = C bonds, respectively [8]. The absorption band at 1434 cm−1 can be recognized as B–O stretching vibrations [24], indicating BA molecule functionalized on the CDs surface after thermal treatment. It can be seen from the FTIR results that all these CDs contain the same stretching vibration bands, evidencing the existence of similar chemical compositions. The FTIR data were further confirmed by the XPS results. The full XPS survey scan display that four CDs are composed of C, O, N and B elements with similar content (Fig. 2f). In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the C 1 s spectra of four CDs (Figure S1) reveal the presence of C = C/C-C (284.7 eV), C–O (285.7 eV), C═O (288.5 eV) and C-B (283.5 eV) bonds [13,24]. The excitation of C-B bonds implies the dehydration process occurs between CDs and BA molecules. These results suggest that CDs contains abundant O, N and B relative groups on their surface/edge. These doping heteroatoms can promote spin–orbit coupling between the excited singlet and triplet states to enhance the ISC processes.

The UV − visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CD dilute aqueous solution were measured in Figure S2 and Figure S3, respectively. The four CD dilute aqueous solution show similar UV absorption and fluorescence emission, implying there exist same luminescent centers in these CDs. In the UV region, the peak at 392 nm is observed, corresponding to the n − π* transitions of C = O bonds [42]. Similar to other reported CDs with uniform optical properties [16], the four CDs display identical excitation-independent fluorescence (FL) features with emission maxima at 420 nm (Figure S3), which indicates the presence of single fluorescence center in these CDs. In contrast to the CDs dilute aqueous solution, CDs@BA composites with various CDs contents exhibit different absorption and FL emissions. In the absorption spectra (Fig. 3 a), the main absorption peak is gradually red-shift and enhanced tails at the longer wavelength become more and more obvious from G-CDs@BA, Y-CDs@BA, R-CDs@BA to NIR-CDs@BA composites, which may be attributed to the CDs aggregation caused by the increased CDs concentration.

In steady-state photoluminescence (Figure S4), G-CDs@BA composites display two significant emission peaks approximately at 430 and 530 nm, of which the later almost coincides with their phosphorescence spectra (Fig. 3b). When G-CDs@BA composites are fully dissolved in water, the peak at 530 nm nearly disappears accompanied by the disappearance of phosphorescence, while the emission centered at 430 nm still exists in fluorescence spectrum (Figure S5). Considering that phosphorescence can be quenched by dissolved oxygen in water and solvent-assisted relaxation, we conclude that the peak at 530 nm in the photoluminescence spectra is mainly derived from phosphorescence, which is supported by our previous work [44]. Fluorescence/phosphorescence behaviors of BA powders were investigated as shown in Figure S6. BA matrix exhibited very weak fluorescence/phosphorescence compared to that of BA@CDs at room temperature, which indicates that the high-efficiency luminescence mainly comes from the contribution of CDs rather than BA.

As the mass ratio of CDs to BA increases, the fluorescence peak clearly shifts from 430 to 600 nm (Figure S4). Similarly, huge red-shift ranging from 530 to 750 nm in phosphoresce spectra is also observed as shown in Fig. 3b-e. From G-CDs@BA to NIR-CDs@BA, the optimal excitation is gradually red-shifted to long wavelengths, meanwhile phosphorescent emission changes from unimodal to multimodal emission, implying the existence of multiple triplet-excited states in the R-CDs@BA and NIR-CDs@BA composites [45]. Moreover, the time-resolved phosphorescence decay spectrum of G-CDs@BA exhibits a two exponentially decay lifetime with average lifetimes of 1.66 s, while for Y-CDs@BA, R-CDs@BA and NIR-CDs@BA, they can be fitted into three exponentially decay lifetimes with average lifetimes of 1.18, 0.016 and 0.0004 s (Fig. 3f and Table S1), respectively, further confirming the existence of multiple triplet-excited states in these CDs. Due to the reabsorption effect and quenching effect at high concentration, the phosphorescence lifetime of CDs@BA composite decreased with increasing the CDs concentration to a high level. The emission redshift and new life component (τ3) suggest the formation of aggregation state. The aggregation of CDs was verified by the TEM imaging (Figure S7). The TEM characterization illustrates that the distance of CDs in BA becomes shorter while increasing the CDs concentration from G-CDs@BA to NIR-CDs@BA, resulting in large aggregates in NIR-CDs@BA. To quantitatively estimate the phosphorescence emission, the absolute phosphorescent quantum yield for G-CDs@BA, Y-CDs@BA, R-CDs@BA and NIR-CDs@BA were measured to be 38.2, 20.8, 1.6 and 0.51%, respectively. The energy gaps between lowest singlet (S1) and triplet (T1) excited states (ΔEST) can be quantitatively determined to be 0.587, 0.107, 0.234, and 0.414 eV, respectively. These small ΔEST values may allow ISC, and consequently emitting phosphorescence.

In addition, the CD1 was synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of levofloxacin with nitrogen heterocyclic structure, and then composited with BA substances at different concentrations under heat-melt treatment. CD1@BA exhibited aggregation-induced red shift behavior (Figure S8), similar to that of CDs prepared from lycorine, implying heterocyclic structure play a role for phosphorescence. In addition to own structure CDs with heterocyclic structure, the BA also plays a role for aggregation-induced phosphorescence red-shift. CDs did not exhibit any phosphorescence in pure solid state, due to their significant aggregating induced luminescence quenching. In the presence of BA, CDs could fully passivate in annealed BA matrix, resulting in a uniform surface state, which avoids aggregation-induced quenching to some extent.

Different from the color-tunable phosphorescence reported previously [43], the particle sizes of the four CDs here are nearly identical, effectively ruling out the quantum confinement effect. In addition, similar elemental composition and stretching vibration bands were detected between the four CDs, which excluding possibility of phosphorescence induced by the surface oxidation state. In four CDs@BA composites, the morphology and surface composition of the CDs did not change significantly, while the distance between them changed significantly. On the basis of the above and analyses, a possible mechanism for wide color-tunable phosphorescence of CDs is illustrated in Fig. 4 . Under the thermal treatment condition, the CDs are in situ formed and fully passivated in annealed BA matrix, resulting in a uniform surface state, which avoids aggregation-induced quenching to some extent. With the increase of CDs contents in BA, the interactions between closely adjacent CDs become stronger as the CDs concentration increases, resulting in energy splitting and form new aggregation states. As a result, the phosphorescence of CDs can continuously shift from green to NIR color.

To further prove aggregation-induced phosphorescence red-shift, we performed control experiment. When the Y-CDs@BA, R-CDs@BA and NIR-CDs@BA composites were re-embedded in excess BA under heat-melt treatment, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of all samples undergone blue-shift, which are similar to that of G-CDs@BA composites (Figure S9). After the CDs is re-embedded into BA, the distance between nano-particles is increased and the aggregation effect disappears, resulting in the phosphorescence of CDs reverting to the surface state-dominated emission. In addition, the G-CDs@BA, Y-CDs@BA, R-CDs@BA and NIR-CDs@BA composites exhibited the phosphorescence even when placed in a bare environment for more than 30 days (Figure S10a), which indicates its good stability. Furthermore, four samples can be activated repeatedly without a significant loss in the phosphorescence intensity, (Figure S10b), indicating a high optical stability and signal coherence.

Based on the aggregation-induced phosphorescence red-shift behavior of CDs, we further developed methanol-triggered color-changing properties. As show in Fig. 5 a,b, G-CDs@BA composites exhibit unprecedented and unique solvent induced redshift phosphorescence phenomenon that color change from green to red after spraying methanol. Besides, fluorescence changing behavior from green to yellow transition was also observed in Fig. 5c,d. Based on these behavior, various dynamic color-changing anti-counterfeiting patterns such as a “star”, “lightning” and “number” (Fig. 5e) could be readily fabricated using methanol as the ink print on the predesigned masks, which shows great potential for application in dynamic anti-counterfeiting. After spraying a small amount of solvent, numerous hydrogen bonding interactions between CDs and BA are destroyed, and CDs aggregation occurs in matrix, and then the aggregate state emission gradually become dominant, leading to the obvious solvent-triggered redshift in emissions. To verify the above analyses, UV–vis spectra of G-CDs@BA composites before and after spraying methanol were measured. As shown in Figure S11, after spraying a small amount of methanol, the main absorption peak is gradually red-shift and enhanced tails at the longer become more and more obvious, implying CDs aggregation.

In summary, we have exploited a facile aggregation-induced emission strategy for the preparation of CDs@BA composites with color fine-tunable and wide-range phosphorescence covering from green to NIR. It has been revealed that the tunable phosphorescence color originates from aggregation-induced multiple splitting energy levels, meanwhile, in-situ formed CDs with uniform surface state avoids aggregation-induced quenching to some extent. Further, we have explored a noble solvent-triggered evolutionary discolorized CDs-BA system and demonstrated their potential applications in information security. Our work provides a facile strategy for realizing wide-range and fine-tunable phosphorescence in CD system and is expected to promote the development of novel stimulus-responsive smart materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002152, 62005106 and 61922091), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Zhenjiang-Modern Agriculture (NY2021007), the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0040/2019/A1, 0128/2020/A3, 0131.2020.A3 and 0073/2019/AMJ), Fund from the University of Macau (SRG2019-00163-IAPME), Special fund for Science and technology innovation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022610) and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (Grant No.KYCX22_3629). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Weiwei from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142339.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sunlight-activated long-persistent luminescence in the near-infrared from Cr3+-doped zinc gallogermanates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Pan, Y.Y. Lu and F. Liu, Sunlight-activated long-persistent luminescence in the near-infrared from Cr3+-doped zinc gallogermanates, Nat. Mater. 11 (2011) 58-63.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong Phosphorescence of Water-Soluble Organic Nanoparticles for In Vivo Afterglow Imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhen, Y. Tao, Z. An, P. Chen, C. Xu, R. Chen, W. Huang and K. Pu, Ultralong Phosphorescence of Water-Soluble Organic Nanoparticles for In Vivo Afterglow Imaging, Adv. Mater. 29 (2017).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Time-Gated Imaging of Latent Fingerprints and Specific Visualization of Protein Secretions via Molecular Recognition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, Q. Ma, H. Liu, Y. Wang, H. Shen, X. Hu, C. Ma, Q. Yuan and W. Tan, Time-Gated Imaging of Latent Fingerprints and Specific Visualization of Protein Secretions via Molecular Recognition, Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 12764-12770.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Lim, W. Shen and Z. Gao, Carbon quantum dots and their applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (2015) 362-381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 0D/2D Z-scheme heterojunctions of Zn-AgIn5S8 QDs/α-Fe2O3 nanosheets for efficient visible-light-driven hydrogen production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhang, B. Mao, D. Li, Y. Liu, F. Li, W. Dong, T. Jiang and W. Shi, 0D/2D Z-scheme heterojunctions of Zn-AgIn5S8 QDs/α-Fe2O3 nanosheets for efficient visible-light-driven hydrogen production, Chem. Eng. J. 417 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-dots-mediated highly efficient hole transfer in I-III-VI quantum dots for photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Li, Y. Liu, B. Mao, L. Li, H. Huang, D. Zhang, W. Dong, Z. Kang and W. Shi, Carbon-dots-mediated highly efficient hole transfer in I-III-VI quantum dots for photocatalytic hydrogen production, Appl Catal B-Environ 292 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in carbon dots: from the perspective of traditional quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, H. Huang, W. Cao, B. Mao, Y. Liu and Z. Kang, Advances in carbon dots: from the perspective of traditional quantum dots, Mater. Chem. Front. 4 (2020) 1586-1613.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Time-Dependent Phosphorescence Colors from Carbon Dots for Advanced Dynamic Information Encryption

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tan, Q. Li, S. Meng, Y. Li, J. Yang, Y. Ye, Z. Tang, S. Qu and X. Ren, Time-Dependent Phosphorescence Colors from Carbon Dots for Advanced Dynamic Information Encryption, Adv. Mater. 33 (2021) e2006781.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Induction of long-lived room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots by water in hydrogen-bonded matrices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Li, M. Zhou, M. Yang, Q. Yang, Z. Zhang and J. Shi, Induction of long-lived room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots by water in hydrogen-bonded matrices, Nat. Commun. 9 (2018) 734.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational Synthesis of Solid-State Ultraviolet B Emitting Carbon Dots via Acetic Acid-Promoted Fractions of sp(3) Bonding Strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xu, Q. Liang, Z. Li, V.Y. Osipov, Y. Lin, B. Ge, Q. Xu, J. Zhu and H. Bi, Rational Synthesis of Solid-State Ultraviolet B Emitting Carbon Dots via Acetic Acid-Promoted Fractions of sp(3) Bonding Strategy, Adv. Mater. 34 (2022) e2200011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N, B-Codoping Induces High-Efficiency Solid-State Fluorescence and Dual Emission of Yellow/Orange Carbon Dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, Q. Li, J. Zheng, Y. Yang, X. Liu and B. Xu, N, B-Codoping Induces High-Efficiency Solid-State Fluorescence and Dual Emission of Yellow/Orange Carbon Dots, ACS Sustain Chem. Eng. 9 (2021) 2224-2236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright high-colour-purity deep-blue carbon dot light-emitting diodes via efficient edge amination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yuan, Y.-K. Wang, G. Sharma, Y. Dong, X. Zheng, P. Li, A. Johnston, G. Bappi, J.Z. Fan, H. Kung, B. Chen, M.I. Saidaminov, K. Singh, O. Voznyy, O.M. Bakr, Z.-H. Lu and E.H. Sargent, Bright high-colour-purity deep-blue carbon dot light-emitting diodes via efficient edge amination, Nat. Photonics. 14 (2019) 171-176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Universal Strategy for Activating the Multicolor Room-Temperature Afterglow of Carbon Dots in a Boric Acid Matrix

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, W. Zhou, Z. Zhou, H. Zhang, X. Zhang, J. Zhuang, Y. Liu, B. Lei and C. Hu, A Universal Strategy for Activating the Multicolor Room-Temperature Afterglow of Carbon Dots in a Boric Acid Matrix, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58 (2019) 7278-7283.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Xu, C. Dong, J. Xu, S.u. Rehman, Q. Wang, V.Y. Osipov, K. Jiang, J. Wang and H. Bi, Fluorinated carbon dots/carboxyl methyl cellulose sodium composite with a temperature-sensitive fluorescence/phosphorescence applicable for anti-counterfeiting marking, Carbon. 189 (2022) 459-466.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intercrossed carbon nanorings with pure surface states as low-cost and environment-friendly phosphors for white-light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, Y. Liu, X. Song, H. Wang, H. Gu and H. Zeng, Intercrossed carbon nanorings with pure surface states as low-cost and environment-friendly phosphors for white-light-emitting diodes, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 54 (2015) 1759-1764.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection
